title: Courageous Leadership
description: Outcome-focused leaders who step into uncertainty, take principled risks, and give others permission to do the same, and protect innovation from political or organizational backlash.
whyItMatters: Transformation requires leaders who not only set direction but also show vulnerability, back experimentation, and protect their teams when the path forward is unclear.
howToUseIt: Model candor by acknowledging uncertainty.
Sponsor bold ideas that align with mission and values.
Publicly celebrate attempts that don't succeed but produced valuable learning.
reflectionPrompt: What would courageous leadership look like in my context today?

title: Systems Mindset
description: An approach that encourages seeing interconnections, feedback loops, and unintended consequences, rather than focusing only on isolated parts.
whyItMatters: Public sector challenges rarely sit neatly in one department. A systems mindset helps leaders anticipate ripple effects, address root causes, and design more resilient interventions.
howToUseIt: Use mapping tools to visualize relationships.
Ask 'what else does this affect?' before acting.
Invite voices from outside the immediate issue space.
reflectionPrompt: Where are we treating a symptom instead of addressing a system?

title: Public Value Co-Creation
description: Public value co-creation is about moving beyond consultation to true partnership. It engages citizens, communities, and stakeholders as collaborators in shaping policy, services, and outcomes. Instead of government acting as sole designer and provider, value emerges through shared dialogue, experimentation, and decision-making.
whyItMatters: When people co-create, they see themselves reflected in the solutions. This builds trust, legitimacy, and ownership of change. It also surfaces lived experience and practical wisdom that institutions often miss—leading to more relevant, effective, and equitable outcomes.
howToUseIt: Convene citizen panels or assemblies to co-design new service models.
Use participatory budgeting or community labs to shape priorities.
Build feedback loops where citizens remain engaged throughout the process, not just at the start.
Pair co-creation efforts with transparent communication about how input shaped decisions.
reflectionPrompt: Where could involving citizens directly in shaping solutions create more trust and better outcomes?
examples: Iceland's Better Reykjavik platform (citizens propose and vote on projects).
Paris Participatory Budget (one of the world's largest citizen-driven funds).
Brazil's Porto Alegre participatory budgeting (classic co-creation model).

title: Cross-Agency Integration
description: Cross-agency integration is about building coordinated structures, governance, and shared platforms that bridge silos. Instead of citizens having to navigate multiple agencies separately, integration creates a seamless experience across departments, levels of government, or even sectors. It moves from fragmented service delivery to joined-up systems.
whyItMatters: Citizens experience government as one entity, not as separate ministries. Without integration, services feel confusing, repetitive, and inefficient. Cross-agency collaboration reduces duplication, streamlines delivery, and enables holistic responses to complex social issues.
howToUseIt: Develop shared service platforms such as 'one-stop' portals or integrated case management systems.
Establish cross-agency governance tables with shared accountability.
Use data-sharing agreements and interoperability standards to connect systems.
Start with high-value life events (e.g., birth, retirement, moving) where integration is most impactful.
reflectionPrompt: Where are citizens currently forced to 'stitch together' services on their own, and how could we integrate that experience?

title: Continuous Learning
description: Continuous learning embeds structured feedback loops into the everyday work of government. It creates rhythms of reflection, evaluation, and adjustment, ensuring that policies, services, and strategies evolve in response to evidence and experience. Learning becomes an ongoing process rather than a one-time activity.
whyItMatters: Transformation is never finished. Without learning, systems become brittle and outdated. A culture of continuous learning makes governments more adaptive, resilient, and capable of responding to complexity. It shifts the focus from proving success to improving practice.
howToUseIt: Conduct regular after-action reviews to capture lessons from both successes and failures.
Incorporate citizen and frontline feedback into ongoing policy updates.
Share learning across teams and agencies through communities of practice.
Use developmental evaluation to track progress in real time and adapt accordingly.
reflectionPrompt: How do we currently capture and act on lessons learned—and what would make that process more routine and useful?
examples: US Department of Veterans Affairs uses after-action reviews to improve services.
Scotland's Improvement Service embeds continuous learning in local government.
World Bank Adaptive Learning Projects emphasize iteration in fragile contexts.

title: Innovation Funds
description: Innovation funds are dedicated budgets set aside for experimentation, prototyping, and scaling new ideas. By carving out fiscal space specifically for innovation, they signal commitment to transformation and provide teams with resources that aren't tied to traditional, risk-averse budget cycles.
whyItMatters: Annual budgeting processes often penalize risk-taking and cut projects before they have a chance to demonstrate impact. Innovation funds protect and legitimize experimentation, ensuring that promising initiatives can move beyond the idea stage and into practice.
howToUseIt: Create innovation 'line items' in ministry budgets to support pilots.
Use staged funding (seed → prototype → scale) tied to learning milestones.
Encourage cross-departmental access to the fund to spark collaboration.
Pair financial support with evaluation mechanisms to build an evidence base.
reflectionPrompt: If you had a small pot of protected funding tomorrow, what bold experiment would you start?
examples: Canada's Impact and Innovation Fund (supports experimental policy approaches).
UK GovTech Catalyst (funds innovative technology solutions to public challenges).
Singapore's GovTech Hive (provides dedicated resources for agile prototyping).

title: Leveraging External Resources
description: Leveraging external resources means intentionally partnering with organizations outside government (universities, nonprofits, private firms, and international networks) to bring in expertise, funding, and technology. These partnerships expand capacity, accelerate innovation, and enable governments to do more than they could alone.
whyItMatters: No single institution can solve today's complex challenges. By tapping into external knowledge and resources, governments avoid reinventing the wheel, reduce costs, and access cutting-edge tools and ideas. It also signals openness, making public transformation more inclusive and collaborative.
howToUseIt: Build partnerships with universities for applied research and data analysis.
Co-develop platforms or tools with private sector and civic tech partners.
Seek external funding (e.g., philanthropy, international organizations) to support innovation pilots.
Join cross-national networks to share knowledge and access resources.
reflectionPrompt: What external partners could bring knowledge, funding, or technology that would amplify our impact?
examples: UK's Alan Turing Institute + Office for National Statistics co-developing data science platforms.
GovTech Singapore partnering with industry to prototype new digital services.
Bloomberg Philanthropies Innovation Teams funding city innovation capacity worldwide.

title: Adaptive Governance
description: Adaptive governance replaces rigid, top-down control with iterative structures that emphasize feedback, learning, and responsiveness. Instead of locking in fixed plans, it builds mechanisms for regular adjustment and course correction. This allows governments to stay responsive to shifting realities.
whyItMatters: Public systems operate under conditions of uncertainty and complexity. Static governance structures struggle to keep pace. Adaptive governance enables leaders to adjust priorities quickly, coordinate across silos, and remain accountable while still flexible. It is the antidote to paralysis in fast-changing environments.
howToUseIt: Create steering groups that revisit and update priorities on a regular cycle.
Build short feedback loops between frontline practice and strategic decision-making.
Use scenario planning and horizon scanning to anticipate change.
Institutionalize 'pause and reflect' checkpoints in policy cycles.
reflectionPrompt: Where in our current governance structures could shorter feedback loops make us more responsive?
examples: New Zealand's Policy Project integrates iterative policy design with learning cycles.
European Union's Horizon Scanning Groups adjust funding and priorities based on emerging risks.
US FEMA's Incident Command System adapts roles and decisions dynamically during crises.

title: Scaling Pathways
description: Scaling pathways are structured routes for moving promising pilots and innovations beyond small experiments into broader adoption, policy integration, or cultural transformation. They intentionally design the journey from 'prototype' to 'mainstream,' ensuring that lessons learned inform adaptation and that resources, governance, and accountability mechanisms grow alongside the initiative.
whyItMatters: Many innovations die in the 'pilot graveyard.' Without pathways for scaling, transformative ideas remain isolated experiments with limited impact. Clear, supported pathways prevent promising work from stalling and allow governments to embed new practices into the fabric of public service delivery.
howToUseIt: Define scaling strategies early (regional rollout, policy integration, or cultural embedding).
Build governance structures that support expansion and sustain learning.
Align funding streams with scaling stages (prototype → rollout → mainstream).
Use evidence and evaluation to adapt interventions as they grow.
reflectionPrompt: What promising pilots in our system are ready to scale—and what pathways could carry them into lasting impact?
examples: UK Government Digital Service scaled GOV.UK from a pilot to a single national platform.
Chile's Laboratorio de Gobierno moved service prototypes into permanent departmental practices.
Estonia's e-Residency scaled from an experiment in 2014 to a globally recognized program.

title: Shared Vision
description: Shared vision is the collective creation of a compelling picture of the future that spans agencies, sectors, and political cycles. It's more than a statement; it's a co-owned roadmap that links diverse actors to a common purpose, anchored in outcomes that matter to citizens.
whyItMatters: Political leadership and administrative priorities change, but transformation requires continuity. A co-created vision provides stability through transitions, helping sustain commitment even when leaders or governments shift. It also fosters alignment, reduces duplication, and strengthens legitimacy by rooting direction in shared values.
howToUseIt: Facilitate visioning sessions that involve multiple agencies, partners, and citizens.
Anchor the vision in measurable outcomes that matter to people, not just institutions.
Express the vision in accessible, visual, and narrative forms to make it memorable.
Refresh it periodically to keep it alive while preserving continuity.
reflectionPrompt: How can we make our vision durable enough to survive leadership changes while still flexible enough to adapt to new realities?
examples: Scotland's National Performance Framework (anchored in wellbeing outcomes, sustained across parties).
New Zealand's Living Standards Framework (provides a cross-government compass for policy).
Canada's Federal Sustainable Development Strategy (multi-year, outcome-based roadmap).

title: Collaboration & Trust
description: Collaboration and trust are not byproducts of working together—they are deliberately cultivated through openness, transparency, and reciprocity. Building trust across silos means creating the conditions where agencies, leaders, and frontline staff feel safe to share information, pool resources, and work toward common goals.
whyItMatters: Without trust, collaboration is superficial at best and destructive at worst. Trust is the foundation for genuine cross-boundary cooperation, enabling shared accountability, faster problem-solving, and resilience in the face of uncertainty.
howToUseIt: Leaders should model openness by sharing information and decision rationales.
Establish joint working groups where responsibilities and credit are shared.
Use transparency tools (open data, shared dashboards) to reduce hidden agendas.
Invest in relationship-building—time spent connecting often unlocks cooperation later.
reflectionPrompt: Where in our work is trust the missing ingredient—and what's one step we could take to strengthen it?
examples: Estonia's X-Road data infrastructure builds trust through secure, transparent data exchange.
US Interagency Council on Homelessness convenes 19 federal agencies with shared accountability.
New Zealand's Auckland Co-Design Lab fosters trust between government and communities.

title: Strategic Capacity-Building Investments
description: Strategic capacity-building investments strengthen the innovation muscles of government by equipping public servants with new skills, tools, and experiences. Through training, secondments, and fellowships, staff gain exposure to systemic approaches, design methods, and adaptive practices that allow them to lead transformation from within—rather than relying solely on external consultants.
whyItMatters: Lasting transformation requires in-house capacity. Without it, innovation remains episodic and dependent on outside expertise. Building systemic skills across the public service ensures governments can continuously adapt, design, and deliver in a changing environment.
howToUseIt: Offer training in design thinking, systems change, and agile methods.
Create secondment opportunities across agencies, communities, or sectors.
Develop fellowships that bring in external expertise while growing internal champions.
Build communities of practice to sustain learning and peer support.
reflectionPrompt: How are we intentionally building our system's innovation muscles—and where are we still outsourcing instead of investing in our people?
examples: UK Policy Lab trains civil servants in design and experimentation methods.
Canada's Public Service Fellowship Program places innovators in departments to grow capacity.
Australia's APS Academy embeds systemic and adaptive learning into public service training.

title: Strong Networks
description: Strong networks connect practitioners, leaders, and communities across organizational and sectoral boundaries. Through communities of practice, professional associations, and peer-to-peer learning, networks enable people to share tools, insights, and support. They create the connective tissue that sustains collaboration and spreads innovation long after individual projects end.
whyItMatters: Projects have timelines, but transformation requires continuity. Networks keep momentum alive, build collective intelligence, and help ideas travel across silos and jurisdictions. They also foster a sense of belonging that can inspire persistence even in the face of bureaucratic resistance.
howToUseIt: Support communities of practice for ongoing peer learning.
Invest in platforms (digital or in-person) that enable knowledge exchange.
Recognize and reward network participation in performance systems.
Leverage networks to scale innovations by spreading stories and practices.
reflectionPrompt: What networks are we part of—or should we build—that could help sustain transformation beyond a single project?
examples: OECD Observatory of Public Sector Innovation (OPSI) convenes global practitioners.
Canadian Free Agents Program built a network of public servants working across departments.
European Social Network connects professionals in social services across 30+ countries.

title: Bright Spots
description: Bright spots are the parts of the system where innovation, improvement, or positive outcomes are already happening. By identifying and amplifying these successes, governments can show that change is possible within existing constraints. Bright spots act as proof points, inspiring others and providing models for replication.
whyItMatters: Transformation often feels overwhelming or unrealistic. Highlighting bright spots shifts the narrative from 'it can't be done' to 'it's already happening.' This builds momentum, boosts morale, and provides tangible examples to guide wider adoption.
howToUseIt: Use data to identify outliers performing above the norm.
Study and document what makes these successes work.
Share stories broadly to inspire replication across agencies.
Provide resources to help scale or adapt bright spots in new contexts.
reflectionPrompt: What bright spots in our system could we shine a light on to show that transformation is not only possible but already happening?

title: Positive Deviance
description: Positive deviance is a problem-solving approach that identifies outliers who succeed against the odds and studies what they do differently. Instead of importing external solutions, it looks within the system for practices and behaviours that already work—then adapts and scales them for broader impact.
whyItMatters: Systems often contain hidden solutions in plain sight. By surfacing and amplifying these practices, governments and communities can generate authentic, context-specific innovations that people are more likely to adopt, since they come from peers rather than outside experts.
howToUseIt: Identify 'positive deviants' through data or observation (outliers performing better than the norm).
Study their practices to understand what drives success.
Share stories and involve outliers as peer teachers and mentors.
Support adaptation of practices across different contexts.
reflectionPrompt: Where in our system are people already succeeding against the odds—and how could their practices inspire wider change?

title: Shared-Measurement Systems
description: Shared measurement systems provide a common set of metrics, indicators, and dashboards that multiple actors agree to use when tracking progress. Rather than each agency or partner measuring outcomes differently, shared systems create alignment and foster a collective sense of accountability for achieving impact.
whyItMatters: When everyone measures success differently, collaboration falters. Shared measurement keeps actors focused on the same goals, enables comparison and learning, and builds trust by making progress transparent. It shifts attention from individual outputs to collective outcomes.
howToUseIt: Convene partners to co-design meaningful, outcome-focused indicators.
Build shared dashboards that make progress visible to all stakeholders.
Use common reporting frameworks to reduce duplication.
Incorporate both quantitative data and qualitative insights from citizens.
reflectionPrompt: What outcomes could we measure collectively that would create accountability and alignment across our partners?
examples: StriveTogether in the US uses shared measurement for cradle-to-career education outcomes.
Scotland's National Performance Framework aligns agencies to wellbeing indicators.
UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) provide a global shared measurement system across nations.

title: Safe-to-Fail Spaces
description: Labs and sandbox environments where teams can try out new ideas without the expectation of guaranteed success. These spaces lower the stakes, making experimentation possible.
whyItMatters: In government, fear of failure often stifles creativity. Safe-to-fail spaces create psychological safety, allowing people to test, learn, and adapt without damaging reputations or wasting resources.
howToUseIt: Create pilot projects with clear boundaries and limited scope.
Frame them explicitly as learning experiments.
Share results (including 'failures') transparently as insights, not mistakes.
reflectionPrompt: Where in your work could you create a space where learning is more valuable than immediate success?
examples: Denmark's MindLab (pioneering cross-ministerial design lab).
Ontario's Behavioural Insights Unit (tests interventions with randomized trials).
Singapore's Regulatory Sandbox (safe space for fintech innovation).

title: Communities of Service
description: Communities of Service reframe service delivery as a collective, relational practice rather than a sequence of organizational handoffs. Instead of treating services as products delivered by individual programs or departments, this approach recognizes that real services are sustained by networks of people—frontline workers, managers, specialists, partners, and community actors—who continuously coordinate, adapt, and learn together in response to real needs.
whyItMatters: Many public services fail not because of poor policy or design, but because coordination, trust, and shared responsibility break down across boundaries. Program-centric models fragment accountability and learning. Communities of Service address this by building shared ownership for outcomes, strengthening continuity and resilience across organizational change, enabling faster adaptation through peer learning and mutual support, and shifting services from compliance-driven delivery to purpose-driven practice.
howToUseIt: Identify the community already delivering the service (often invisible in org charts).
Create intentional spaces for connection: learning circles, case reviews, cross-boundary forums.
Align incentives, governance, and data to support collective outcomes, not siloed performance.
Use Service Blueprints to reveal where communities interact with formal systems.
Invest in facilitation and trust-building as core service infrastructure.
reflectionPrompt: Who is already part of the community delivering this service—even if we don't formally recognize them? Where do relationships matter more than rules in making this service work? How might we support the community of service, not just the formal program?
examples: Denmark's MindLab (pioneering cross-ministerial design lab).
Ontario's Behavioural Insights Unit (tests interventions with randomized trials).
Singapore's Regulatory Sandbox (safe space for fintech innovation).

title: Exit Ramps for Stale Work
description: Exit ramps for old work are formal mechanisms to stop, pause, or sunset initiatives, programs, or tasks that no longer create value. They make it legitimate—and expected—to discontinue work that consumes energy, attention, and resources without advancing outcomes.
whyItMatters: Transformation requires capacity. When everything continues indefinitely, nothing truly changes. Without clear exit ramps, organizations keep 'watering fake flowers'—maintaining the appearance of progress while starving work that could create real value. Letting go is not failure; it is stewardship.
howToUseIt: Establish regular stop–start–continue reviews for initiatives and portfolios.
Require explicit sunset clauses or renewal decisions for new work.
Tie continuation to evidence of value, not effort or history.
Publicly recognize leaders and teams who responsibly stop low-value work.
reflectionPrompt: What are we still watering that looks alive—but no longer bears fruit?

title: Authorizing Ambiguity
description: Authorizing uncertainty across cycles is the explicit granting of permission—by senior political and administrative leaders—for teams to act, learn, and adapt without full clarity, precedent, or certainty, while being protected from disruption caused by leadership changes, electoral cycles, or shifting priorities.
whyItMatters: Systemic transformation unfolds in conditions of uncertainty and over time. Without clear authorization to experiment and protection from political or leadership turnover, teams default to risk avoidance, short-term thinking, or performative compliance. This enabler creates the psychological and institutional safety needed to learn forward while maintaining legitimacy across cycles.
howToUseIt: Secure explicit sponsorship statements that legitimize experimentation and learning.
Establish cross-party, cross-ministry, or cross-administration agreements for priority transformations.
Anchor initiatives in enduring public outcomes rather than individual leaders or programs.
Document intent, principles, and learning so continuity survives personnel change.
reflectionPrompt: What work in our system would move faster—and more honestly—if people knew they were permitted to act under uncertainty and protected across political and leadership cycles?
examples: Denmark's MindLab (pioneering cross-ministerial design lab).
Ontario's Behavioural Insights Unit (tests interventions with randomized trials).
Singapore's Regulatory Sandbox (safe space for fintech innovation).
